title: Handling conflicting datetime formats
short-description: Events from syslog or catch-all TCP inputs often mix several timestamp formats under one sourcetype, defeating the default parser. An INGEST_EVAL that tries strptime() against each candidate format and takes the first match keeps every event's _time accurate.
details:
**Problem.** A single sourcetype receiving events with mixed timestamp formats — typical for syslog aggregators and catch-all TCP listeners — defeats Splunk's default parser, which locks to one format per sourcetype. Events that do not match silently get the wrong `_time` and land in the wrong time bucket.

**Fix.** Set `DATETIME_CONFIG=CURRENT` and use an `INGEST_EVAL` cascade of `strptime()` attempts to recognise each format per event. No custom `datetime.xml`, no US-bias in date ordering, and every event's `_time` is correct regardless of which format the producer emitted.

title: Stitching dates in filenames with times in events
short-description: Some applications put the date in the filename and only the time in each log line. Use INGEST_EVAL to pull the date from source with a regex replace, concatenate it with the time from _raw, and parse the combined string with strptime().
details:
**Problem.** Some applications write the date into the filename or directory and only the time-of-day into each log line. Splunk's timestamp parsing reads only from `_raw`, so every event in the file gets stamped at the current date instead of the intended one.

**Fix.** `INGEST_EVAL` pulls the date out of `source` with a `replace()` regex, concatenates it with the time from `_raw`, and parses the combined string with `strptime()`. Events are timestamped correctly without touching `datetime.xml`, and a parse failure falls back to `CURRENT_TIME` so bad filenames never drop the event.

title: Dynamic extraction of indexed fields
short-description: Files with a loose `attribute=value` convention — like splunkd's metrics.log — can be fully indexed without enumerating field names. A REGEX transform with REPEAT_MATCH=true and WRITE_META=true captures bare, single-quoted, and double-quoted values in one pass, making tstats searches dramatically faster.
details:
**Problem.** Logs that follow a loose `key=value` convention — splunkd's `metrics.log` is the canonical case — have no first-class extractor in Splunk. Writing a REGEX per field is tedious and brittle, and any new key the producer starts emitting quietly disappears.

**Fix.** A REGEX transform with `REPEAT_MATCH=true` and `WRITE_META=true` walks each event and captures every pair — bare, single-quoted, and double-quoted — as an indexed field in one pass. Combined with `SEGMENTATION=search` to suppress redundant major breakers, `tstats` on the result runs roughly forty times faster than the schema-on-the-fly equivalent.

title: Additional license usage metadata
short-description: License attribution in license_usage.log is limited to sourcetype/host/index, and indexes like _internal never appear there at all. Attach len(_raw) as an indexed field on every event so tstats gives you accurate, dimensional ingestion volumes across any time range.
details:
**Problem.** License attribution in `license_usage.log` is limited to sourcetype, host, and index. Internal indexes like `_internal` and `_metrics` never appear there at all, and any dimension the license log does not track (app, team, source path) cannot be reported on without re-parsing every raw event.

**Fix.** Apply an `INGEST_EVAL` under `[default]` that writes `len(_raw)` to a `_event_length` indexed field on every event. A single `tstats sum(_event_length)` query gives you dimensional ingestion volumes across any time range, including the internal indexes, with no raw-event parsing at search time.

title: Write license.log into _metrics index
short-description: Historical license analysis from license_usage.log is slow because every row is an unstructured string. Clone each Usage event with CLONE_SOURCETYPE, extract the dimensions into indexed fields, and route the copy to _metrics so mstats can answer capacity questions in milliseconds.
details:
**Problem.** Historical license analysis from `license_usage.log` is slow because every row is an unstructured string in `_internal`. Questions like "which host/sourcetype pair drove last quarter's growth?" take minutes or hours to parse.

**Fix.** `CLONE_SOURCETYPE` each Usage row into a metric-compliant copy. A REGEX extracts `b` into `_value` and the dimensions (host, source, sourcetype, index, indexer, pool) into indexed fields, then `INGEST_EVAL` routes the copy to `_metrics`. `mstats` answers capacity questions in milliseconds; the original event still flows to `_internal` untouched.

title: Dropping indexed fields from INDEXED_CSV
short-description: INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS=CSV turns every column into an indexed field — fast, but disastrous for bucket size when columns are high-cardinality or unused. Drop the noisy columns from _meta with INGEST_EVAL `:=null()` and fall back to search-time extraction, keeping buckets small without losing the columns that matter.
details:
**Problem.** `INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS=CSV` indexes every column as a field regardless of whether any search uses it. High-cardinality or never-queried columns bloat the `tsidx`, and in extreme cases the indexed-fields bucket is larger than the raw-text bucket — wrecking retention against the storage estimates in the sizing guide.

**Fix.** Keep the columns you actually search as indexed fields; use `INGEST_EVAL` with `:=null()` to remove the rest from `_meta`, and add a search-time `EXTRACT-` for the dropped columns so they remain reachable via schema-on-the-fly. `tstats` speed where it matters, storage penalty only where it does not.

title: Advanced Masking of Data with SHA1
short-description: Masking sensitive fields usually destroys them. Here the masked event and a second 'map' event in a protected index carry matching SHA1 hashes: low-privilege users see only the redacted version, while privileged users join the two at search time to reverse the mask.
details:
**Problem.** Sensitive fields — emails, passwords, internal IDs — must be hidden from most users but recoverable by privileged operators. Splunk's built-in Anonymize Data destroys the original value, and lookup-based approaches cannot prove the mapping was built at ingest time.

**Fix.** Replace the sensitive value with a short SHA1 hash in the normal event, and `CLONE_SOURCETYPE` a second "map" event into a protected index carrying the same hash alongside the original. Low-privilege users see only the redacted version; users with access to both indexes can join the two at search time to reverse the mask, without any lookup infrastructure.

title: Simple masking data
short-description: The simple pattern for selective redaction: CLONE_SOURCETYPE the event, apply a SEDCMD to the clone that partly masks email addresses and fully masks passwords, and route the original and the clone to indexes with different access controls.
details:
**Problem.** You need selective masking — a redacted copy for general access and an unredacted copy for security auditors — but do not want the complexity of hash-mapping infrastructure.

**Fix.** `CLONE_SOURCETYPE` the event, apply a `SEDCMD` to the clone that partly masks email addresses (first and last characters preserved for support triage) and fully masks passwords, then route the original and the clone to indexes with different access controls. Two lines of `SEDCMD` and a clone transform is the whole pattern.

title: Export and import data
short-description: Moving sample events between Splunk instances usually needs access to the source files. An export search packs _time, index, host, source, sourcetype, and _raw into a single CSV line, and an import sourcetype unpacks it back into the correct metadata fields on the other side.
details:
**Problem.** Moving sample events between Splunk instances — for development, consulting, training, or reproducing a customer issue — normally requires access to the raw source files. In practice that access is blocked by network segmentation or data-governance policy.

**Fix.** An export search packs `_time`, `index`, `host`, `source`, `sourcetype`, and `_raw` into a single CSV line per event using a `%%%` separator and a `^^^END^^^` line delimiter. An import sourcetype on the target uses REGEX and `INGEST_EVAL` to unpack the encoding, restoring each event exactly as if it had been ingested natively.

title: Split splunk forwarding
short-description: A single parsing pipeline caps around 20 MB/s, and concentrating that through one tcpout stanza can saturate a single indexer (the 'laser beam of death'). Set _TCP_ROUTING with INGEST_EVAL to spread events randomly across several tcpout target groups so load balances across multiple indexer connections.
details:
**Problem.** A single Splunk parsing pipeline tops out at roughly 20 MB/s, and concentrating that through one `tcpout` stanza saturates a single indexer connection — the "laser beam of death" that stalls event-delay queues on one indexer while the rest of the cluster sits idle. When the input stream cannot be sharded across forwarder pipelines, adding pipelines does not help.

**Fix.** On a heavy forwarder, set `_TCP_ROUTING` with an `INGEST_EVAL` that appends `random()%N` to a target-group name. Define N matching `[tcpout:group_0]`, `[tcpout:group_1]` … stanzas in `outputs.conf`, each pointing at the full indexer pool. Each event picks a group at random and the single stream fans out across N independent TCP connections.

title: Enrich splunkd.log
short-description: The _internal index is usually one of the largest on a platform, so every splunkd search pays full schema-on-the-fly cost. Extract log_level, component, thread_id, and thread_name as indexed fields at ingest — plus a custom segmenter that skips the timestamp tokens — to make those searches tractable.
details:
**Problem.** The `_internal` index is usually one of the largest on any Splunk deployment, and every field in `splunkd.log` — `log_level`, `component`, `thread_id`, `thread_name` — is extracted on the fly at search time. Routine diagnostics on `_internal` pay full schema-on-the-fly cost and take minutes on busy clusters.

**Fix.** A REGEX transform extracts `log_level`, `component`, `thread_id`, and `thread_name` as indexed fields at ingest. A companion `segmenters.conf` stanza skips the date and time tokens (they never help search) and disables intermediate major breakers, keeping the `tsidx` small. `tstats count by log_level component` now runs in a fraction of the time.

title: Enrich splunkd_access.log
short-description: Every REST call against splunkd lands in splunkd_access.log — high volume, entirely unstructured. Parse source IP, method, response code, bytes, and duration at ingest, then split the URL's query string into per-parameter indexed fields (rest_param_*) so latency and usage analysis moves to tstats.
details:
**Problem.** `splunkd_access.log` records every REST call against `splunkd` — high volume, entirely unstructured. Performance troubleshooting ("which endpoint is slow?", "which user hit the rate limit?") requires a full `_raw` scan every time.

**Fix.** A single REGEX parses source IP, user, method, URL, response code, bytes, and duration into indexed fields. A second REGEX with `REPEAT_MATCH=true` splits the URL's query string into per-parameter indexed fields (`rest_param_*`), staged through the host metadata key as a workaround for operating on `_meta`. Latency and usage analysis moves cleanly into `tstats`.

title: Shard data with splitByIndexKeys
short-description: Very large indexes cause SmartStore churn when sparse searches pull small amounts of data from many buckets. Set indexes.conf `splitByIndexKeys=source,sourcetype` and raise the hot-bucket count so data with the same keys lands together, giving bucket elimination real leverage.
details:
**Problem.** Very large indexes cause SmartStore churn when sparse searches pull small amounts of data from many buckets. SmartStore localises whole buckets, so a query that needs one row per bucket can end up downloading gigabytes of unrelated data.

**Fix.** Set `splitByIndexKeys=source,sourcetype` in `indexes.conf` and raise `maxHotBuckets`. Buckets are no longer split only by time; events with the same key combination land together, so bucket elimination on `source` or `sourcetype` excludes most of the index before any localisation happens. The platform docs say this is metrics-only — it is not.

title: Measure ingestion costs before onboarding
short-description: Measure the real cost of a new data source before committing it to production. A staging pipeline counts bytes per host/source/sourcetype without persisting the events, so you can hand the onboarding team concrete volumes instead of guesses.
details:
**Problem.** Quoting license impact for a new data source before onboarding normally means guessing. A wrong number either scares off the integration or blows the license budget later in the quarter.

**Fix.** A staging pipeline reads the candidate source, counts bytes per host, source, and sourcetype into a metrics index, and then routes the events to `nullQueue` without committing them to a real index. You hand the onboarding team concrete dimensional numbers instead of estimates.

title: Selective routing
short-description: Different downstream systems need different subsets of the same stream. Use CLONE_SOURCETYPE with per-copy filters and _TCP_ROUTING to send the original to one target, a redacted copy to another, and drop the rest — all from a single heavy-forwarder config.
details:
**Problem.** Different downstream systems need different subsets of the same event stream — a SIEM might want only security-relevant events, a billing integration might want only license metadata. Filtering on the forwarder is brittle and per-source; filtering downstream wastes bandwidth and compute.

**Fix.** Use `CLONE_SOURCETYPE` with per-copy `INGEST_EVAL` filters, plus `_TCP_ROUTING` targeting different `tcpout` groups. Each event can be duplicated, filtered, redacted, or routed independently from a single heavy-forwarder config — one source of truth, many downstream consumers.

title: Loading json-docker logs
short-description: Docker's default log driver wraps every line in a JSON envelope like `{"log":"...","stream":"stdout","time":"..."}`. Parse it with INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS=JSON, then copy the `log` field back into _raw and drop the wrapper with INGEST_EVAL so the event indexes as if Docker never touched it.
details:
**Problem.** Docker's default log driver wraps every line in a JSON envelope like `{"log":"...","stream":"stdout","time":"..."}`. Splunk does not recognise this natively, and reading with `INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS=JSON` indexes the log text itself as a high-cardinality `log` field — bloating the `tsidx` and breaking downstream search patterns. Most teams bolt on FluentD as a workaround.

**Fix.** Parse with `INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS=JSON`, then use `INGEST_EVAL` to copy the `log` field into `_raw` and drop the `log` and `time` indexed fields. The event indexes exactly as it would have before Docker wrapped it, with `stream` preserved as a useful dimension. With `force_local_processing=true`, this even works from a universal forwarder.

title: Find IP addresses
short-description: Searching for an IP like 10.20.30.1 builds a LISPY that matches every event containing the tokens 1, 10, 20, and 30 — wildly inefficient. Extract validated IPv4 addresses into an `ip` indexed field at ingest, with look-around anchors that reject false matches like 1.2.3.4.5, so queries can eliminate on `ip::` directly.
details:
**Problem.** Searching for an IP like `10.20.30.1` generates a LISPY `[ AND 1 10 20 30 ... ]` that matches every event containing any of those tokens. Splunk filters the false positives back out via schema-on-the-fly, wasting enormous amounts of parsing effort.

**Fix.** A REGEX transform with look-around anchors extracts validated IPv4 addresses into an `ip` indexed field at ingest, rejecting things that look like IPs but are not (`1.2.3.4.5`, `300.x.y.z`). Searches can now eliminate on `ip::10.20.30.1` at the lexicon stage, before any event parsing happens.
